summary: I was born in Stockton, California, specifically on Woodword Island in the delta. My father was a farmer there until he passed away in 1932. Stockton was where I grew up and worked on farms and vineyards, especially during the Great Depression when jobs were scarce but Japanese workers were valued.

The Great Depression hit us hard, especially after my father died in 1932. Jobs were tough to come by, but Japanese workers like me were still able to find work, even if wages were low. It was a time when everyone was poor except the big landowners, and many families struggled to put food on the table.

When Franklin D. Roosevelt was elected president before 1932, things began to improve slowly. Roosevelt's administration helped create programs like the WPA that provided jobs and support during the Depression. Around 1936, under his leadership, the vineyard work started to pay off, and life got a bit better for us.

The Works Progress Administration, or WPA, was one of the programs Roosevelt created to help people during the Depression. It helped provide jobs and support for many struggling families, including Japanese workers and others in California. It was part of the gradual improvement we saw starting in the mid-1930s.

After 1932, many people from Oklahoma came to California, first settling in Bakersfield and Fresno before moving north to areas like Stockton. Their experience was similar to what was shown in the movie The Grapes of Wrath, with many living in poor conditions and struggling to find work. They were new to farming here and had a tough time adapting compared to the Japanese who already knew the work.

People from Arkansas, along with those from Oklahoma, moved to California during the Depression years, seeking work and better lives. Like the Oklahomans, they faced hardships and lived in poor conditions, often in shacks without proper facilities. Their story was much like the one told in The Grapes of Wrath, highlighting the struggles of migrant farm workers.

The Grapes of Wrath was a movie that told the story of Oklahomans who came to California during the Depression. Their experience was just like what I saw—people living in shacks, dirty from lack of bathing, and struggling to survive. It was a familiar story because many migrants, including those from Oklahoma and Arkansas, faced these hardships.

I remember the morning of Pearl Harbor attack clearly; it was early Sunday around 8 a.m. in 1941. Just days before, I had taken a trip to Los Angeles and Terminal Island, not knowing war was imminent. The attack was the turning point that led to fear and suspicion against Japanese Americans like me.

After Pearl Harbor, the FBI started picking up Japanese Americans suspected of disloyalty. They investigated groups like Heimusha Kai, which we supported by donating, claiming it was aid for soldiers' families. The FBI wanted to see records, and while they arrested some leaders, many of us were spared, though rumors about arrests caused fear.

Heimusha Kai was an organization that collected donations to support Japanese soldiers' families during the war. Even Nisei like me contributed, and we kept records that the FBI reviewed after Pearl Harbor. We never claimed to aid soldiers directly, but the government tolerated it as family support, though it brought suspicion.

Japan was fighting in Manchuria during the time leading up to World War II, which was part of the context for organizations like Heimusha Kai. The conflict there was one reason for heightened suspicion of Japanese Americans, especially Kibei who had strong ties to Japan. It was a tense period that affected our community deeply.

When the war started, the War Production Board controlled resources like tractors and cars. I had a tractor I bought for $2,500, but the government froze prices and usage, so I couldn't sell it or use it freely. They gave me back exactly what I paid when they took it, and even controlled car sales, only allowing sales to farmers with priority.

My family and I were from Hiroshima Prefecture originally, and we associated with others from the same area. This connection was important socially and culturally, especially when we lived in California. It helped us maintain ties and support each other through difficult times, including before and after the war.

Sumo wrestling was popular in our community when I was young, around the late 1920s to mid-1930s. We had sumo teams in places like Lodi, Stockton, and Sacramento, sponsored by the Seinen Kai organization. Tournaments were held on holidays like the 4th of July, and it was a big event that brought people together from different towns.

Seinen Kai was the organization that sponsored sumo wrestling teams in our area, including Lodi, Stockton, and Sacramento. They organized tournaments and helped keep the sport alive among Japanese Americans. Being part of Seinen Kai connected us socially and culturally during those years before the war.

I visited Griffith Park on a trip to Los Angeles just days before Pearl Harbor. It was part of a sightseeing tour with my family, and it was the first time I had seen places like Griffith Park and the Hollywood Bowl. That trip was memorable because it was just before everything changed with the war.

During a visit to Los Angeles before the war, I saw the Hollywood Bowl for the first time. It was part of a pleasant sightseeing trip that included Griffith Park and a ferry ride to Terminal Island. That day stands out in my memory because it was so peaceful before the war disrupted our lives.

Although Kinmon Gakuen wasn't mentioned directly in my story, it was an important Japanese language school in the community where many Japanese Americans, including families like mine, maintained their language and culture. Schools like Kinmon Gakuen helped us stay connected to our heritage during the difficult times of the Depression and war.
